var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'GM Crops' paragraph points out that GM planting 'declined slightly in 2015', shows 'little sign of an upturn', and that the main GM markets are 'largely saturated' — a dim near-term outlook for GM business. That matches A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt 'What is argued here?' wants the paragraph's central business claim — the bottom line the author wants you to take away, not a side detail about 1996 or the names of the crops.

steps.title: Locate the signal sentences
steps.text: Two contiguous sentences carry the argument: 'global planting of GM crops declined slightly in 2015, after two decades of rapid growth, and there is little sign of an upturn in the near future. The markets... are largely saturated... and few others are poised for introduction.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Stripped to plain language: GM growth has stalled, the existing markets are full, and there is no new market about to open. That is a bleak short-term outlook for the GM business — not a long-term verdict, just a near-future one.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Saturated market' = a market where almost everyone who would buy is already buying — no room left to grow. 'Upturn' = a turn upward in a downward trend. 'Poised for introduction' = ready to be launched soon. 'Dim prospects' = poor outlook.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'short-term business prospects looking dim' matches 'declined slightly... little sign of an upturn... markets saturated'. B says new seeds will STIMULATE the market — the paragraph says the opposite. C 'definitive end of the GM success story' is too strong; the text says decline and saturation, not extinction. D 'long-term profitability not in doubt' contradicts the saturation evidence and overshoots into the long term, which the paragraph never settles.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The argument is about a near-term business problem — decline, saturation, no new markets — which is exactly what 'short-term prospects looking dim' captures.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'expansion of the market for GM seeds' in the opening sentence and assume the paragraph predicts more growth coming.
distractors.why_wrong: That sentence describes what the deal would DEPEND on, not what the author thinks will happen. Steps 2-3 show the rest of the paragraph immediately undercuts the optimism with 'declined slightly' and 'few others poised for introduction'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'declined slightly' plus 'little sign of an upturn' and conclude the GM story is finished.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Definitive end' is much stronger than 'declined slightly... near future'. The text describes a short-term plateau, not a permanent end — step 3's near-future framing rules out an absolute verdict.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to flip the negative tone and assume the paragraph reassures readers that GM is still a sound long-term bet — the recent 'blockbuster deal' would point that way.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph never argues for long-term profitability — it only inventories present headwinds. Step 5's option-by-option pairing shows D adds a long-term verdict the text does not make.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'What is argued here?' questions in short ELF paragraphs, find the sentence with a CONTRAST word ('Yet', 'However', 'But') — the author's actual argument almost always lives on that side of the contrast, not in the setup sentence before it.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: The first sentence of a business paragraph often describes the optimistic FRAMING, while the real argument lives in the 'Yet/However' that follows. Read past the framing before you pick.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The paragraph notes that markets are saturated only 'in the countries that are politically and agriculturally hospitable to GM' — implying that other countries are NOT hospitable, i.e. some countries oppose GM. That is what C captures.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: 'What else is implied?' tells you the explicit argument (Q31) is already covered — now look for a SECONDARY claim that the author hints at without spelling out. Pay attention to qualifying phrases, not the headline.

steps.title: Locate the implication phrase
steps.text: The key phrase is the qualifier: 'in the countries that are politically and agriculturally hospitable to GM'. The author would not need to add this qualifier unless the OTHER countries — the ones not hospitable — also exist and matter.

steps.title: Restate the implication in plain English
steps.text: If markets are saturated only where countries are 'politically hospitable to GM', then by definition there are countries that are NOT politically hospitable — that is, countries that politically reject or restrict GM seeds. That is the unstated claim.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Politically hospitable' = the government and laws welcome it. 'Agriculturally hospitable' = the climate and farming culture suit it. The pairing tells you GM is blocked either by politics OR by farming conditions in some places.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says 'no longer any real demand' — too absolute; the paragraph shows saturation in existing markets, which actually means demand. B says the potential was 'misjudged from the start' — the paragraph praises 'two decades of rapid growth', so the start was not a misjudgement. C 'some countries remain opposed' is the direct unpacking of 'politically hospitable' — that wording only makes sense if some countries are politically opposed. D 'more attention to environmental aspects' — the paragraph never mentions the environment.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The single word 'politically' in 'politically hospitable' is doing the implying — it presupposes a politically hostile camp, and that is exactly the country-level opposition C names.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'declined' and 'little sign of an upturn' as a verdict that demand has dried up.
distractors.why_wrong: Saturated markets still HAVE demand — they just have no room left to grow. Step 3 shows the implication is about geographic limits, not collapsed demand.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'declined slightly in 2015' and read it back into 1996 — as if the market was overestimated from the start.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph explicitly calls the first two decades 'rapid growth' — that is the opposite of a market that was misjudged at launch. The decline appears only late in the timeline.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to import a familiar GM controversy — environmental risks — when the text mentions 'politically' and 'agriculturally'.
distractors.why_wrong: The text never raises environmental concerns. Step 5 rules D out by absence: 'environmental' does not appear anywhere in the paragraph.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'What else is implied?' items, find the QUALIFYING phrase (a clause that limits the scope of a claim — 'in the countries that...', 'among those who...'). The implication usually lives on the OTHER side of that limit — the cases the qualifier silently excludes.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: When an author qualifies a claim ('politically hospitable countries'), they are doing two jobs at once — stating the claim AND admitting an opposite group exists. The implication is always the second job.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The opening two paragraphs say Mesoamerican murals 'were more narrative than decorative' and 'depict mythological scenes, heroic battles, and life in opulent royal courts'. That is exactly the storytelling claim in A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt restricts you to the OPENING TWO paragraphs and asks for a general claim about the Central American paintings. So you ignore later sections about Cacaxtla and look only for the introductory framing about Maya / Mesoamerican murals.

steps.title: Locate the framing sentence
steps.text: The headline sentence is in paragraph one: 'For the ancient Mesoamericans, murals were more narrative than decorative.' The next sentence backs it up: paintings 'depict mythological scenes, heroic battles, and life in opulent royal courts'.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: 'Narrative rather than decorative' means the murals existed to TELL STORIES, not just to look pretty. The examples that follow — myths, battles, court life — are all story types. So the author's general claim is: these paintings were storytelling.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Narrative' = telling a story. 'Decorative' = there for visual decoration only. 'Mythological' = about myths, gods. The word 'narrative' is the single most important word in the opening — every later option-test runs through it.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'generally meant to tell a story of some kind' is a clean paraphrase of 'more narrative than decorative' plus its examples. B claims they were 'mainly created in two specific regions' — the text names Bonampak (Chiapas) and Calakmul (Campeche) as EXAMPLES, not as the only regions. C claims more striking colours than elsewhere — the text calls them 'brightly coloured' but never compares them to other parts of the world. D claims they were larger than similar paintings elsewhere — the text never makes a size comparison.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The opening's whole framing rests on one contrast — narrative versus decorative — and A is the literal restatement of that contrast.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to over-weight the named places (Bonampak in Chiapas, Calakmul in Campeche) and read them as the GEOGRAPHIC SCOPE of the murals rather than as illustrative examples.
distractors.why_wrong: Named examples illustrate the headline, they do not bound it. Step 5 shows the headline ('more narrative than decorative') is general and not tied to two regions.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading of 'brightly coloured wall paintings' and 'vividly painted murals' makes a colour-comparison feel natural.
distractors.why_wrong: Bright colours are mentioned, but only as a property — never relative to other parts of the world. C invents the cross-region comparison; the text does not make it.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading of 'large-scale paintings' suggests size is what makes them noteworthy.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Large-scale' is descriptive, not comparative — the text never tests these murals against any size baseline. Step 5 catches the absence.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'What is claimed in paragraph X?' questions, find the CONTRAST sentence ('more A than B', 'rather than', 'not just X but Y') — that contrast is almost always the paragraph's headline claim, and the correct option is the cleanest paraphrase of one side of it.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Named places and dates feel like 'the answer' because they're concrete, but they almost always serve as supporting examples for an abstract claim. The claim lives in the framing sentence around them.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The passage says 'little is known about how these paintings were actually made', then introduces Cacaxtla as 'a lesser known' group of murals that 'may provide scholars with some of the most convincing evidence yet'. New evidence from a previously little-explored site — exactly C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The question asks what is said about THE CREATION of Mesoamerican paintings — the making process. So you are looking for sentences about HOW these murals were produced, not about their subjects or colours.

steps.title: Locate the creation-process sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the claim. First: 'Despite the number of well-preserved examples, however, little is known about how these paintings were actually made.' Then: 'A lesser known – but nonetheless dazzling – group of ancient Mesoamerican murals may provide scholars with some of the most convincing evidence yet for how these artworks were created.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Scholars have not known how the murals were made. But a less-studied group of murals — at Cacaxtla, as later sentences reveal — might give the best clues so far. So the story is: gap in knowledge → new evidence from a previously overlooked place.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Lesser known' = not previously studied much. 'Convincing evidence' = strong proof. 'Clues about the process' = hints about HOW something was done. The whole sentence is about FILLING a knowledge gap, not closing it — note the hedging word 'may'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says 'recent evidence from Chiapas and Campeche' — those are the FAMOUS sites (Bonampak, Calakmul) that the text holds up as PUZZLES, not the new source. B says 'much is known' — directly contradicts 'little is known'. C 'possible clues... from a previously unexplored location' matches 'lesser known... may provide... convincing evidence'. D says lack of surviving details makes the process unclear — the text says examples are 'well-preserved', so the gap is in knowledge, not in surviving material.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The passage frames Cacaxtla as a new, less-explored window onto a long-standing gap in scholarship — the textbook shape of 'possible clues from a previously unexplored location'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to map 'Mesoamerican paintings' to the two named sites (Chiapas and Campeche) and assume new evidence is coming from THOSE famous sites.
distractors.why_wrong: Chiapas and Campeche are named to introduce the puzzle, not to host the new evidence. Step 5 shows the new evidence comes from Cacaxtla in Tlaxcala — a different state — which is the 'lesser known' site.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the long list of follow-up details (number of artists, training, travel) and read it as material that has been figured out.
distractors.why_wrong: The list comes right after 'little is known' — it is a list of OPEN QUESTIONS, not answered ones. Step 3 keeps the gap-of-knowledge framing visible.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the phrase 'little is known', it is tempting to attribute the gap to missing material — paintings too worn to study.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly calls the examples 'well-preserved'. The gap is in scholarly understanding, not in surviving evidence — D mis-locates the problem.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-034.technique: On questions about the 'state of knowledge' on a topic, look for the THREE-PART shape: gap ('little is known...') → potential source ('may provide... new evidence') → cautious verb ('may', 'could', 'might'). The correct option preserves the hedged tone — overstating it picks up the wrong option.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: Famous named sites (Bonampak, Calakmul) feel like they should be the source of new evidence because they're concrete. But in this kind of passage they're usually the unsolved-puzzle baseline against which a lesser-known site is introduced.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The text says 'few elite burials or high-quality ceramics have been found' at Cacaxtla or its precursor — meaning the area 'was never very prosperous' — yet they made impressive murals. That gap between modest wealth and ambitious art is exactly B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The question is about Cacaxtla as a PLACE — its wealth, status, scale — not about the murals' artistic content. So filter for sentences that describe the site's prosperity, size, or success.

steps.title: Locate the wealth verdict
steps.text: Two sentences are decisive. 'The fact that few elite burials or high-quality ceramics have been found either there or in a much earlier pyramid complex about a mile west called Xochitécatl suggests that the area was never very prosperous.' And later: 'Although Cacaxtla never became especially powerful...'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Two independent signals: archaeology (no rich tombs, no fancy ceramics) and history ('never especially powerful'). Plain reading: Cacaxtla was a modest, not-rich, not-powerful place. The TENSION the passage flags is that this poor little state produced impressive murals anyway.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Elite burials' = the tombs of wealthy, high-status people; lots of them = a rich society. 'High-quality ceramics' = fancy pottery; again a wealth marker. 'Never very prosperous' = never rich. 'Peripheral state' = a small state on the edge, not at the centre.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says the area was occupied by a 'rich and successful pyramid-building culture' before the palace — the text says the pyramid complex at Xochitécatl ALSO shows no signs of prosperity. B 'despite the impressive murals, the site was neither rich nor successful' matches the two-signal pattern in step 3 exactly. C 'highly successful culture' contradicts 'never very prosperous' and 'never especially powerful'. D 'conquered Xochitécatl to become rich' is invented — the text never mentions a conquest, only a chronological precursor.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The whole point of the Cacaxtla story is the gap between modest material wealth and ambitious art — and B is the only option that pairs the modesty with the murals as a 'despite'.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading of 'pyramid complex' next to Xochitécatl feels grand — pyramids signal wealth in many cultures.
distractors.why_wrong: The text bundles Xochitécatl WITH Cacaxtla as also showing 'few elite burials or high-quality ceramics' — i.e. also not rich. Step 4 grounds the wealth markers in archaeology, not architecture.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the impressive 'large palace' covered with frescoes and infer that a culture capable of such art must have been highly successful.
distractors.why_wrong: Art ambition and material prosperity are NOT the same thing in this passage. Step 3 captures the central tension: ambitious art coexisting with modest wealth — C collapses that tension.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on encountering two named places — Cacaxtla and Xochitécatl — is to fill in a story about how one rose by overcoming the other.
distractors.why_wrong: The text describes Xochitécatl as a 'much earlier pyramid complex' — i.e. chronologically prior — and never mentions a conquest. D invents an event the text does not describe.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On 'What are we told about site X?' questions, hunt for the ARCHAEOLOGICAL EVIDENCE clauses — 'few... have been found', 'no signs of', 'evidence of'. Those are the author's verdict markers; the correct option restates them in plain wealth/status language.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: Impressive art makes readers reach for 'successful culture' as the verdict. In archaeology passages, prosperity is measured by burials and ceramics, not by paintings — the text usually flags the gap explicitly.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: Bonampak's celebrated murals were painted by 'two or three artists', while Cacaxtla's 'Battle Mural' had 'at least six and as many as 13 painters working simultaneously'. So Cacaxtla's defining feature is a much larger team — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The question asks for a COMPARISON: Cacaxtla murals versus great Maya site murals. So find the sentence that puts the two side by side and identify what makes Cacaxtla different, not just what makes it impressive.

steps.title: Locate the comparison sentences
steps.text: Two sentences sit next to each other: 'In the case of the "Battle Mural", she believes there were at least six and as many as 13 painters working simultaneously.' Then: 'By contrast, the spectacular murals at Bonampak... were probably painted by two or three artists.'

steps.title: Paraphrase the contrast
steps.text: Bonampak (a great Maya site) = 2-3 painters per mural. Cacaxtla = 6 to 13 painters on one mural. Same era, similar ambition, but very different team size. The headline difference is HOW MANY PEOPLE painted one wall at once.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Working simultaneously' = working at the same time, in parallel — not sequentially. 'By contrast' = signals a direct comparison with the previous sentence. 'Collaborative work' = work done by many people together. The whole comparison is about team size, not about time, size, or quality.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says each mural took longer — the opposite is implied (more painters in parallel could mean LESS time, not more). B says they were larger and more impressive — the text actually says Bonampak's are 'often called the finest', not Cacaxtla's. C 'each mural was created by a large number of artists' is the direct paraphrase of '6 to 13 painters' versus '2 or 3'. D 'within a very short period of time' — the text says nothing about elapsed time.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The 'By contrast' sentence is doing only one job: pointing out that Cacaxtla used much bigger teams than the great Maya sites. That is the specific feature the question asks for.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the phrase 'taxing and labor-intensive projects', it is tempting to translate that into 'took much longer'.
distractors.why_wrong: More painters working in parallel speeds the work up, not down. The text never compares Cacaxtla's elapsed time with the Maya sites — step 5's comparison stays squarely on team size.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume Cacaxtla's murals were 'larger and more impressive' because the article foregrounds them.
distractors.why_wrong: The text calls Bonampak's murals 'the finest such works yet discovered in the Americas' — i.e. the GREAT Maya sites still hold the size/impressiveness crown. Cacaxtla's distinction is the team, not the scale.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct, given 'painters working simultaneously', is that the work was rushed through quickly.
distractors.why_wrong: The text never describes the timeframe of either Cacaxtla or Bonampak murals. D answers a question the passage does not address — step 5 catches the absence.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'What is specific about X compared with Y?' questions, find the 'By contrast' or 'unlike' sentence. The single feature that sentence puts on either side of the contrast IS the answer — extra adjectives in the surrounding text are noise.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: It is tempting to import the wider 'impressive murals' framing into the comparison answer. The contrast clause names ONE specific difference; the correct option is a paraphrase of that one difference, not a global judgement.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The closing line says Cacaxtla 'never became especially powerful' yet was 'an ambitious state with a great wealth of imagination'. Modest material status plus ambitious creative output — that pairing is exactly 'poor but creative', answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the BEST CHARACTERIZATION of the whole Cacaxtla culture — a two-adjective summary. So look for the sentence that gives the overall verdict, and check that BOTH adjectives in the chosen option match it.

steps.title: Locate the verdict sentence
steps.text: The closing sentence is the headline verdict: Cacaxtla 'never became especially powerful', but 'its inhabitants showed, through their art, that it was... "an ambitious state with a great wealth of imagination claiming to be more cosmopolitan than we have any evidence that it was."' Two clauses, one on each axis.

steps.title: Paraphrase as a two-axis summary
steps.text: Axis 1 (wealth/power): 'never especially powerful', 'never very prosperous', 'few elite burials' → POOR. Axis 2 (cultural output): 'ambitious state', 'great wealth of imagination', impressive murals → CREATIVE. So the verdict pair is: poor on power and wealth, rich on imagination and art.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Peripheral state' = a small state at the edge, not central or dominant. 'Wealth of imagination' = a metaphorical wealth — lots of creativity, not lots of money. 'Cosmopolitan' = connected to many cultures. The pair the text actually settles on is small/peripheral plus imaginative/ambitious.

steps.title: Match against the options as PAIRS
steps.text: A 'influential but short-lived' — the text never says Cacaxtla influenced others, and its mural period 600-900 A.D. is not described as short. B 'successful but isolated' — the text says the OPPOSITE on both halves: not successful (peripheral) and not isolated (at a 'crossroads', culturally connected). C 'aggressive but unified' — the Battle Mural depicts war, but the text never characterizes Cacaxtla itself as aggressive. D 'poor but creative' — 'poor' matches 'never very prosperous' and 'never especially powerful'; 'creative' matches 'wealth of imagination' and 'ambitious state'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The entire closing paragraph is built around exactly this contrast — modest material standing paired with ambitious creative output — and D is the only option whose two halves both land.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the murals as influential and to assume the culture itself influenced wider Mesoamerica.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says Cacaxtla absorbed Maya influences, not that it spread its own. And the mural period spans roughly 300 years — not short-lived. Both halves of A miss.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'great wealth of imagination' and read it as material success, then anchor 'isolated' on the small-state imagery.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly says Cacaxtla was 'at the crossroads' of a connected Mesoamerican world — the opposite of isolated. And 'never especially powerful' rules out 'successful'. Step 5 catches both half-failures.

distractors.why_tempting: The mention of the 'Battle Mural' and 'forces... from several different states' makes 'aggressive' feel apt.
distractors.why_wrong: The mural depicts war as a SUBJECT, not the city as an aggressor. The closing verdict frames Cacaxtla as 'a tiny, peripheral state' asserting identity through art — not as a warlike actor.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'best characterized as X but Y' questions, treat the option as a TWO-PART claim and confirm BOTH halves separately against the passage. If either half misses or is unstated, the option is out — only the option where both halves cite back to the text is correct.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Single-axis options ('influential but short-lived') often nail one adjective and bluff the other. Always test both — the wrong option usually fails on the half you don't double-check.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: Mole rats are 'cold-blooded, cancer-resistant, pain-immune... long-lived' AND now shown to survive without oxygen — a stack of unusual resiliences. That stack is what 'uniquely adapted to endure extreme conditions' captures — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: 'What is IMPLIED?' tells you not to look for a literal claim, but for the cumulative picture the paragraph paints. The author keeps stacking adjectives — that stack is the implication, and the question is what the stack adds up to.

steps.title: Locate the adjective stack
steps.text: The first sentence is the giveaway: 'the cold-blooded, cancer-resistant, pain-immune and conspicuously long-lived rodents also can survive for extended periods of time without oxygen.' Five resiliences in one sentence: temperature, cancer, pain, ageing, oxygen.

steps.title: Paraphrase the implication
steps.text: Stack five separate resiliences onto one animal and the implication is bigger than any single feature: this animal handles biological stresses that other mammals don't. So the author is implying mole rats are an outlier — uniquely tough across many fronts.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Cold-blooded' (here, in a mammal) = highly unusual, signals biological strangeness. 'Cancer-resistant' = does not develop cancers the way most mammals do. 'Conspicuously long-lived' = lives much longer than expected for its size. The repeated word the paragraph builds up to is 'weirder' — the author wants you to read this as cumulative unusualness.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A says they have 'evolved to become even more resilient in recent years' — the paragraph reports that scientists DISCOVERED new resilience, not that the animal evolved. B says they 'mainly use fructose instead of glucose' — the text says they 'use glucose, too' and can SWITCH to fructose; fructose is not their main fuel. C says they would 'function just as well without access to oxygen' — too absolute; they survive an 18-minute deprivation, not a permanent absence. D 'uniquely adapted to endure extreme conditions' is the clean read of the five-resilience stack.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The implication is built up by a stack of resiliences — and 'uniquely adapted to endure extreme conditions' is exactly the umbrella concept the stack adds up to.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'got even weirder in 2017' as the animal CHANGING in 2017 — gaining a new ability.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'got even weirder' is the AUTHOR'S framing of new scientific FINDINGS, not a recent biological evolution. Step 3's distinction between discovery and evolution rules A out.

distractors.why_tempting: If you anchor on the most distinctive new finding (fructose metabolism), it's tempting to read fructose as the animal's primary fuel.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly says 'naked mole rats use glucose in this way, too' — glucose is normal; fructose is the backup when oxygen runs out. B inverts the primary/backup relationship.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to translate 'survive 18 minutes without oxygen' into 'do not really need oxygen'.
distractors.why_wrong: Survival under extreme deprivation is not the same as not needing oxygen. The text frames oxygen-deprivation as a tested LIMIT, not the animal's normal state — C overshoots.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'What is implied?' questions in short paragraphs, look for ADJECTIVE STACKS — three or more unusual properties listed together. The implication is almost always the umbrella concept those properties share, not any single property.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: The most surprising single finding (fructose metabolism, 18 minutes without oxygen) is bait — it pulls readers to a narrow option about that one finding. The implication is usually the broader pattern the finding adds to.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: Newtok's residents 'voted in 2003 to relocate' but 'progress has been slow' because of 'bureaucratic barriers' around approval and funding. So even when the will is there, adapting to climate change runs into red tape — exactly the complexity claim in A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: 'What is implied here about Newtok?' wants the take-away of the paragraph as a case study — what Newtok ILLUSTRATES about responding to climate change, not just what happened in Newtok.

steps.title: Locate the take-away sentence
steps.text: The closing sentence is the case-study verdict: 'Newtok's plight shows that even when a community wants to move, bureaucratic barriers can make the shift difficult.' Before that: residents voted in 2003, but progress is slow due to approval and funding hurdles.

steps.title: Paraphrase the lesson
steps.text: The community AGREED to adapt (vote 2003). The adaptation has STALLED on bureaucracy (approvals, funding, building infrastructure at the new site). The general lesson: adapting to climate effects is harder than just deciding to adapt — the implementation is complex even with consensus.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Bureaucratic barriers' = administrative obstacles — paperwork, approvals, agency processes. 'Plight' = a difficult situation. 'Permafrost' = ground that stays frozen year-round; when it thaws it destabilizes everything built on it. The closing phrase 'make the shift difficult' is the implication you carry forward to the options.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'illustrates the complexity involved in adapting to effects caused by climate change' matches 'bureaucratic barriers can make the shift difficult' — the case study is about complexity in implementation. B 'encouraging example of how to successfully react' — the opposite; the example is a stuck case, not a success. C 'difficulty for locals to agree' — the text says they DID agree (the 2003 vote); the difficulty is external. D 'short-sightedness of most measures' — the text never criticizes the measures as short-sighted, only as slow.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The vote-versus-bureaucracy gap is the heart of the paragraph, and 'complexity of adapting' is the only option that captures HOW the case became stuck — by administrative obstacles, not by disagreement.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'voted in 2003 to relocate' and read the case as a success story — a community taking the right step.
distractors.why_wrong: The vote is only half the story. The second half — 'progress has been slow' — turns this from a success into a case of stuck implementation. Step 3's lesson-paraphrase keeps the stuck-ness in view.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading of 'progress has been slow as residents try to secure approval' could be misread as residents struggling to agree among themselves.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Residents try to secure approval' means they are seeking external approval (from government, agencies) — not approval from each other. The 2003 vote already shows internal consensus. Step 5 catches the misreading.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line about thawing permafrost and rapid coastal erosion, it is tempting to read the paragraph as critical of the measures being taken — as if they're too small.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph never evaluates the relocation as short-sighted. It evaluates the IMPLEMENTATION as slow — a process problem, not a strategy problem.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'What is implied here about case X?' questions, find the sentence that begins 'X's plight shows...' or 'X illustrates...' — the case-study verdict is almost always stated explicitly in one sentence, and the correct option is a paraphrase of that verdict.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: Climate-change passages tempt readers to import generic narratives ('failure to act', 'too late', 'unwilling locals') that the specific paragraph never makes. The right move is to stay inside the case-study verdict sentence and reject options that import an outside narrative.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The paragraph rewrites the old slave-trade story (British-led, Caribbean-bound) — 'now it's becoming clearer that the Portuguese were the main traders, and Brazil was the principal destination'. New numbers and new actors complicate the standard picture, which is exactly A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: 'What is implied here?' on a paragraph that overturns a 'consensus for a long time' — the implication is almost always about how the OLD view is wrong or incomplete. So scan for the contrast between consensus and 'now it's becoming clearer'.

steps.title: Locate the consensus-versus-now contrast
steps.text: The decisive quote is in two halves. Old view: 'The consensus for a long time was that the British were the main slave traders and the Caribbean was the main destination.' New view: 'Now it's becoming clearer that the Portuguese were the main traders, and Brazil was the principal destination for slaves on the Atlantic.' The numbers (5.5 million to Brazil, less than 400,000 to North America) back the new view.

steps.title: Paraphrase the implication
steps.text: The old picture was simple and Anglo-Caribbean. The new picture is Portuguese-led and Brazil-centred, with much larger numbers in the South Atlantic than previously assumed. The implication: recent research is revising the slave-trade story toward a richer, more nuanced picture — different lead actors, different destinations, different volumes.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Consensus' = the long-standing agreed view among scholars. 'Disembarked' = stepped off the ship; here, where slaves ended up. 'Principal destination' = the main place where they arrived. The whole paragraph is structured as old-versus-new, with the new view backed by hard numbers.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'latest research reveals a more complex picture' matches 'now it's becoming clearer... Portuguese... Brazil' over the old British-Caribbean view — a more nuanced, less simple picture. B 'British dominated the Caribbean slave trade during the early period' — the text says the OPPOSITE; the British-led view was the old consensus now being revised, and the paragraph never carves out an 'early period' exception. C 'Portuguese GRADUALLY took over' — implies a chronological transition; the text actually implies the Portuguese were the main traders all along, and the consensus was just wrong. D 'route went via Brazil to North America' — the numbers compare them as separate destinations (Brazil vs. North America), not as stages of one route.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The whole paragraph is a textbook 'revised understanding' shape — old consensus, new findings, different actors, different scale — and 'more complex picture' is the natural umbrella for that revision.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to keep the British-Caribbean piece intact and treat it as a true sub-story even if the Portuguese-Brazil story is also true.
distractors.why_wrong: The text frames the British-Caribbean view as a MISTAKEN consensus being overturned, not as a partial truth surviving alongside the new finding. Step 2's old-versus-new contrast is mutually exclusive in the historian's quote.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Now it's becoming clearer that the Portuguese were the main traders' can read like a description of a TAKEOVER over time.
distractors.why_wrong: 'It's becoming clearer' refers to scholarly understanding shifting, not to history shifting. The Portuguese being the main traders was already TRUE in the 16th-19th centuries — scholars only recognized it later. C confuses historiography with history.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to read 'less than 400,000 disembarked in North America' as part of a Brazil-to-North-America route.
distractors.why_wrong: The text presents the two destination numbers (2+ million in Rio de Janeiro vs. less than 400,000 in North America) as SEPARATE destinations to compare scale — not as stages of a single route via Brazil.

var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On 'What is implied here?' for historiography paragraphs, look for the 'consensus was X... now it's clearer Y' shape. The implication is almost always 'the picture is more complex / has been revised' — A-style umbrella claims usually win over options that defend the OLD consensus or invent a NEW timeline.
var-2022-2-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: When numbers appear (5.5 million, 2 million, 400,000), it is tempting to use them to construct narrow routes or transitions. They are almost always there to BACK the revised picture, not to imply a specific route. Stay on the revision claim.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: The sentence pits 'this increase 31 coincides' against 'something causative is happening' — an 'either-or' between a chance overlap and a real cause. 'Merely coincides' = nothing more than coincides — exactly the side that contrasts with 'causative'. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: This is an ADVERB slot modifying 'coincides'. The whole sentence asks whether the rise in suicidal thoughts (a) just happens to line up in time with social media, or (b) is actually CAUSED by it. The gap word needs to flag the 'just happens to line up' side.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The structure is: 'whether this increase ___ coincides with the rise of social media, OR whether something causative is happening.' The 'whether... or' frames a binary. The blank side must be the weak, non-causal reading; the other side names cause.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is CONTRAST between 'just a chance overlap' and 'real cause'. The adverb in the gap must SHARPEN the chance-overlap reading — make it sound like the timing is the WHOLE story, with nothing more behind it.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Merely' = only, nothing more than; signals that the noun/verb it modifies is the FULL extent — no hidden cause. 'Probably' = likelihood, with no contrast to cause. 'Eventually' = in the end; about time, not relationship. 'Frequently' = often; about how OFTEN, not whether it is causal.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: 'whether this increase MERELY coincides... or whether something causative is happening' — pristine contrast: only a coincidence vs. an actual cause. 'PROBABLY coincides' — odd because the binary partner is causation, not probability. 'EVENTUALLY coincides' — coincidences don't happen 'eventually'; awkward. 'FREQUENTLY coincides' — there is only one rising trend being compared, so 'frequently' makes no sense.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. 'Merely coincides' is the only option that makes the 'chance overlap vs. real cause' binary work — and that binary is the whole point of the opening sentence.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to slot in 'probably' because it sounds cautious and scientific — psychologists hedging their conclusions.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Probably coincides' doesn't pair with 'or whether something causative is happening' — the contrast needs a strength word (only / merely / just), not a probability word. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the bad pairing.

distractors.why_tempting: If you anchor on the 'over the past decade' time frame in the sentence before, 'eventually' feels temporal and fitting.
distractors.why_wrong: Coincidences are POINT-IN-TIME, not gradual — 'eventually coincides' is a contradiction in terms. The temporal framing belongs to the trends themselves, not to whether they coincide.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to read 'increase coincides with' as describing many small coincidences and reach for 'frequently'.
distractors.why_wrong: There is only ONE increase being compared with ONE rise of social media — a single overlap, not a series. 'Frequently coincides' requires repeatable events; step 4's vocabulary check rules it out.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On cloze gaps in a 'whether A or whether B' construction, identify what B says and pick the word for A that makes A the OPPOSITE of B. Here B is 'causative', so A must be 'non-causative' — 'merely' is the textbook word for 'only-coincidental, no cause'.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: Adverb gaps tempt readers to grade options by tone (cautious, scientific) instead of by logical fit with the surrounding 'whether... or' contrast. Plug each option in and read the WHOLE binary before picking.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: The sentence says Twenge analysed 'national 32, with data collected from over 500,000 American teenagers' — a giant dataset of self-reported answers. That is a SURVEY in standard research vocabulary — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: This is a NOUN slot for the source Twenge analysed. The 'with' phrase tells you what the source contains: data collected from 500,000+ teenagers. The gap word has to name a research INSTRUMENT large enough to collect such data — and one that is plural ('national 32, with data...').

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Before: 'By scrutinising national ___' — Twenge is examining a large national-scale source. After: 'data collected from over 500,000 American teenagers' — the source is something that gathers responses from huge numbers of people. Then: agreement with statements like 'The future often seems hopeless' — the data is self-reported attitudes.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is the gap word's TYPE: a research instrument that collects self-reported answers at national scale. That points to questionnaires given to large samples — i.e. surveys. Not articles, not data centres, not programs.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Surveys' = systematic questionnaires given to large samples; the standard tool for national attitude data. 'Journals' = academic publications; you scrutinize them for prior research, not for raw teenager attitudes. 'Centres' = institutions or buildings; not a data source one 'scrutinises'. 'Programs' = curricula or initiatives; not a source of attitude data.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: 'national SURVEYS, with data collected from over 500,000 teenagers' — natural; surveys collect data from large samples. 'national JOURNALS' — articles aren't 'data collected from 500,000 teenagers'. 'national CENTRES' — centres do not collect attitudes; they may host data, but you analyse the data, not the centre. 'national PROGRAMS' — programs deliver interventions; data comes from evaluating them, not from the programs themselves.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The 'with data collected from over 500,000 teenagers' apposition is the giveaway — only 'surveys' is the kind of national instrument that collects self-reported answers from such a large sample.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'national 32' as 'national academic literature' — Twenge scrutinising published research.
distractors.why_wrong: The clause that follows is 'with data collected from over 500,000 American teenagers' — that describes a raw data source, not a body of literature. Step 2's apposition test rules out journals.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'national' and pair it with 'centres' as in national research centres.
distractors.why_wrong: You scrutinize DATA, not institutions. And research centres don't themselves contain the 500,000-teen dataset — they HOUSE such data at best. Step 4's vocabulary check catches the category mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'national' as government-led and think of school programs, 'programs' feels institutional and fitting.
distractors.why_wrong: Programs are interventions, not data instruments. You don't collect attitude data 'from' programs — you collect it FROM students, via surveys. D names the wrong category of thing.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On NOUN gaps with an APPOSITIVE phrase ('national X, with data collected from Y people'), use the apposition as a definition. The gap word has to be the kind of thing whose IS the description in the apposition — here, the thing that 'collects data from 500,000 teenagers'.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: Surface words like 'national' tempt readers toward institutional nouns (centres, programs, agencies). The deciding test is always: 'does the appositive phrase make sense as a description of THIS noun?' — only surveys pass that test here.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: Twenge 'concedes' that her data 'does not 33 causality' — she admits the limits of her own findings. The standard concession in social science is that correlation does not PROVE causality. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: This is a VERB slot. 'As Ms Twenge herself concedes, this does not ___ causality.' She is acknowledging a limit of her own study. The verb must capture what her data CANNOT do with respect to causality — and the next sentence ('another factor may lie behind') gives the standard 'correlation is not causation' tell.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Before: 'As Ms Twenge herself concedes' — a self-correction; she is admitting something. After: 'Another factor may lie behind... sad teenagers... more likely to seek refuge in their phones' — this is the textbook correlation-vs-causation caveat: maybe the depression CAUSES the phone use, not the other way around.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is the standard methodological humility move: 'I found a CORRELATION, but I cannot show CAUSATION.' The verb in the gap is the one that distinguishes correlational findings from causal claims — i.e. the verb that means 'establish for certain', because that's what correlations FAIL to do for causality.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Prove' = establish with certainty; what correlations cannot do for causation. 'Depend on' = rely on; doesn't fit (her data does not 'depend on causality'). 'Exclude' = rule out; she is admitting she cannot CONFIRM causation, not that she cannot rule it out — the opposite move. 'Account for' = explain; she is conceding the strength of the inference, not what is being explained.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: 'this does not PROVE causality' — classic correlation/causation caveat, matches the 'another factor may lie behind' continuation. 'does not DEPEND ON causality' — nonsense; data doesn't depend on causality. 'does not EXCLUDE causality' — the OPPOSITE of the concession; she'd be saying she can't rule causation OUT, but the next sentence walks back her causal claim, not the no-cause hypothesis. 'does not ACCOUNT FOR causality' — vague; one says 'X accounts for Y', not 'X accounts for causality'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The 'concedes' framing plus the 'another factor may lie behind' continuation is the textbook 'correlation does not prove causation' move — and 'prove' is the only verb that fits the standard caveat.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Depend on' is a common verb in research writing and sounds vaguely methodological.
distractors.why_wrong: Data does not 'depend on' causality — that pairing is ungrammatical in research writing. Step 4 anchors the verb to the actual concession: correlation cannot ESTABLISH causation.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember that good studies should 'rule out' alternative explanations, 'exclude' feels like the right move.
distractors.why_wrong: Twenge is saying she cannot CONFIRM causation, not that she cannot RULE IT OUT. Step 3's standard caveat shape — 'correlation is not causation' — is about positive proof, not exclusion.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'account for' as an academic verb that fits anywhere — and 'account for causality' sounds vaguely deep.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Account for X' means 'explain X'. Twenge is not failing to explain causality; she is failing to ESTABLISH it. Step 5's plug test catches the meaning mismatch.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On 'X does not ___ causality' inside a 'concedes' frame, recognize the textbook correlation-vs-causation move. The verb is almost always PROVE, ESTABLISH, or DEMONSTRATE — the verbs of certainty that correlational data cannot deliver.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: Methodological verbs (depend on, exclude, account for, control for) all sound academic and tempt readers who don't slow down to plug each option in. Plug-and-test on every option until exactly one reads as standard research English.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: One group quit Facebook; a '___ group continued browsing... as usual'. The textbook label for the second group in an experiment is the CONTROL group — the one kept on the normal condition for comparison. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: This is a NOUN/MODIFIER slot describing a research group. The structure is 'a randomly selected group quit Facebook for a week; a ___ group continued browsing the site as usual.' Two groups, one given the treatment, the other kept normal — that is a classic controlled experiment design.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Before: 'asked a randomly selected group of adults to quit Facebook for a week' — this is the TREATMENT group. After: 'a ___ group continued browsing the site as usual' — this is the comparison group, kept on the baseline behaviour. The gap word labels that role.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is structural: treatment versus control in an experiment. The control group is the one held to the normal condition so researchers can compare against the treatment group's outcome. That is the universal label — 'control group' is the textbook term in any English-language research paper.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Control group' = the group kept at the baseline so the treatment effect can be isolated. 'Service group' = not a research term. 'Research group' = the team of researchers, not the participants. 'Teenage group' = the study is on adults, not teens — and even if it were, the contrast is treatment vs. baseline, not age.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: 'a CONTROL group continued browsing the site as usual' — the textbook term; describes exactly what the second group does. 'a SERVICE group' — meaningless in this context. 'a RESEARCH group continued browsing' — the research group are the SCIENTISTS, not browsers. 'a TEENAGE group' — but the study is on adults; this contradicts the earlier description.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Control group' is the universal English research label for the comparison group held at the baseline — and the sentence structure makes that role unmistakable.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to look for any plausible adjective without testing it — 'service' sounds vaguely organisational.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Service group' is not a research term and has no meaning in an experimental design. Step 4's vocabulary check rules it out by category.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember that scientists are often called 'researchers' and 'research group' is a familiar collocation, C feels safe.
distractors.why_wrong: A research group is the team running the study, not the participants. The sentence is about participants — 'a research group continued browsing' would mean the SCIENTISTS browsed, which is nonsense in context.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to import the teenager focus from the rest of the article, even though this specific 2016 study sample is described as adults.
distractors.why_wrong: The study sentence explicitly says 'a randomly selected group of ADULTS to quit Facebook'. So a 'teenage group' contradicts the sample. Step 5's plug test catches the mismatch.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-034.technique: On cloze gaps describing the SECOND group in a study ('one group did X; a ___ group continued as usual'), the answer is almost always CONTROL — the textbook English research label for the baseline group. Recognize the treatment-vs-control shape on sight.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: Adjective options that sound vaguely scientific (service, research) bait readers who don't know the research vocabulary. The plug-test against the explicit role — 'continued browsing the site as usual' — is decisive.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: The closing paragraph reverses the gloom: research suggests social-networking sites can 'indeed ___ happiness if used to engage directly with other users'. The reversal needs a POSITIVE verb — and 'promote happiness' is the standard English collocation. Answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap
steps.text: This is a VERB slot with 'happiness' as the object. The whole final paragraph is a TURN: 'Not all studies are equally damning. Research suggests that social-networking sites can indeed ___ happiness if used to engage directly with other users.' The 'indeed' and 'not all damning' flag a POSITIVE claim — so the verb's effect on happiness is positive.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Before: 'Not all studies are equally damning' — preparing a counter-claim to the prior gloom. 'Research suggests that social-networking sites can indeed ___ happiness if used to engage directly with other users' — the conditional 'if used to engage directly' frames the positive scenario. After: 'rather than just to covet glossy photos' — contrast with passive scrolling, which is the negative scenario.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is REVERSAL plus CONDITIONAL: the previous paragraphs argued social media depresses people; this last paragraph says when used a certain way, it does the OPPOSITE — it increases or fosters happiness. So the verb must mean 'increase / foster / advance' happiness.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Promote' (a feeling) = encourage, foster, boost — the standard English verb for 'increase a positive outcome'. 'Demand' happiness = require it as a precondition; doesn't fit. 'Preserve' happiness = keep existing happiness from being lost; the sentence is about generating happiness, not protecting it. 'Diminish' happiness = reduce it; the OPPOSITE of the reversal the paragraph is making.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: 'social-networking sites can indeed PROMOTE happiness if used to engage directly' — natural; standard collocation 'promote happiness/well-being'. 'DEMAND happiness' — sites don't demand happiness; nonsense. 'PRESERVE happiness' — odd; the sentence is about positive effects of using the site, not about protecting existing well-being. 'DIMINISH happiness' — flips the reversal back to negative; clashes with 'indeed' and 'not all damning'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Indeed' and 'not all damning' signal the positive reversal, and 'promote happiness' is the English collocation that lands the reversal in one verb.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Demand' is a strong verb and the article has been emphatic about social media effects.
distractors.why_wrong: Sites don't 'demand' happiness as a condition — happiness is the OUTCOME the sites can produce, not a precondition. Step 4's collocation check rules it out.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Preserve happiness' sounds gentle and positive — a fit with the reversal tone.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph is about SITES PRODUCING happiness through direct engagement, not about shielding pre-existing happiness from loss. The 'if used to engage directly' clause frames an active, generative effect — not a protective one.

distractors.why_tempting: If you missed the 'not all damning' reversal and stayed in the gloomy frame of the earlier paragraphs, 'diminish' feels consistent.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Indeed' explicitly signals AGREEMENT with a positive claim, and 'not all damning' has already turned the paragraph. 'Diminish happiness' contradicts both signals. Step 2's framing reading catches the reversal.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On cloze verb gaps with the structure 'NOT all X are bad. Research suggests Y can INDEED ___ Z if used in way A', recognize the REVERSAL frame. The verb must be POSITIVE on Z, and 'promote' is the universal English verb for 'increase a beneficial outcome'.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Readers who don't notice the 'not all damning' turn keep the gloomy frame and pick a negative verb (diminish). The phrase 'indeed' is the giveaway — it signals confirmation of a positive claim, never of a continued negative one.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: Nazi consul Gyssling could demand cuts 'not only... to films to be shown in Germany, but in all versions of the films to be shown internationally' — different conditions imposed on the German and international cuts. That is exactly the special-conditions pressure in A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The question asks specifically HOW the Nazis exerted pressure on Hollywood producers. So look for the MECHANISM — what concrete leverage they used — not for the general fact that they had influence.

steps.title: Locate the mechanism sentence
steps.text: Two sentences carry the mechanism. 'Article 15 of a law regulating film imports made it clear that Berlin could ban a company from working in Germany, if it produced a film that was offensive to German sensibilities.' Then: 'He could not only demand cuts or changes to films to be shown in Germany, but in all versions of the films to be shown internationally.'

steps.title: Paraphrase the leverage
steps.text: The leverage is a SCREENING-CONDITION threat: if a studio wanted to keep selling films in Germany at all, it had to accept the consul's cuts — both for the German version AND for international releases. So the pressure took the form of conditions imposed on the films screened in different markets.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Demand cuts or changes' = require edits before release. 'All versions of the films to be shown internationally' = different release versions for different markets. 'Considerable influence' = a lot of de facto power. The mechanism word the passage uses is 'demand cuts/changes' — i.e. imposing specific conditions on what could be shown to which audience.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'special conditions for films screened for German and other audiences' — direct match to 'cuts... in films to be shown in Germany, but also in all versions of the films to be shown internationally'. B 'withdrew financial support' — no Nazi financing of Hollywood is mentioned; the leverage was market access, not money. C 'used influence in discussions with American political authorities' — the consul dealt with STUDIOS, not Washington. D 'obstructed international success' — too vague and unidirectional; the pressure was about CUTS, not about blocking distribution.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The leverage was screening conditions — specific cuts demanded both for the German market and for international versions — and A is the only option that names that mechanism.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume that a government's economic leverage comes through withdrawing funding — the standard pressure shape in many contexts.
distractors.why_wrong: Hollywood was financing itself; the Nazi leverage was MARKET ACCESS (the threat of being banned from selling films in Germany), not Nazi money being withdrawn. Step 3's paraphrase grounds the leverage in market access.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Nazi consul in Los Angeles' and picture diplomatic-level pressure on the US government.
distractors.why_wrong: The text describes Gyssling working directly with studios ('Hollywood went out of its way to work with him'), not with the US government. The pressure pathway is studio-to-consul, not diplomat-to-Washington.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading of 'in all versions of the films to be shown internationally' suggests OBSTRUCTING international success.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says the consul demanded CUTS to international versions — not that he blocked international distribution. The films still played internationally; they just played in censored form. Step 5 catches the difference between conditions and obstruction.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'In what way did X put pressure on Y?' questions, find the LEVERAGE clause — the sentence that names what X could threaten or require. The mechanism (cuts to films, ban from market, blacklist of figures) is almost always stated explicitly; the correct option is a clean restatement of that mechanism.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: Generic pressure narratives (withhold funding, lobby government, block exports) feel plausible but rarely match the specific mechanism a passage describes. Always pin the answer to a literal phrase in the text.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: Instead of showing Gyssling the door, 'Hollywood went out of its way to work with him' — he was 'listened to intently' and projects were dropped to avoid 'his disapproval'. American film producers extended him understanding and accommodation, exactly what C says.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The question is about how the American film industry RESPONDED to Gyssling's activities — what the producers' attitude and behaviour toward him looked like. So find the sentences that describe Hollywood's reaction, not Gyssling's demands themselves.

steps.title: Locate the reception sentences
steps.text: Three short sentences carry it. 'Instead of showing Gyssling the door, Hollywood went out of its way to work with him.' 'Urwand chronicles several occasions on which he was listened to intently.' 'Some projects did not happen because of fear of his disapproval.'

steps.title: Paraphrase the reception
steps.text: Hollywood didn't push back — it accommodated. Producers listened carefully to his concerns, dropped projects to avoid offending him, and worked with him as a partner rather than treating him as a hostile presence. In short: they treated him with notable understanding.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Go out of its way' = make extra effort. 'Listened to intently' = attentive, serious listening. 'Showing someone the door' = sending them away — Hollywood did the opposite. 'Disapproval' = the consequence the studios feared. The collective vocabulary paints accommodation, not resistance.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'initially met with strong opposition' — opposite of the text; Hollywood went out of its way to work with him from early on. B 'impact was less than often thought' — the text actually emphasises the impact (projects dropped, cuts made internationally). C 'treated with great understanding by American film producers' is a direct paraphrase of 'went out of its way to work with him' plus 'listened to intently' plus 'fear of his disapproval'. D 'strengthen financial ties' — the GOAL Gyssling wanted was censorship; the financial ties were Hollywood's motive, not his stated purpose.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The whole 'how Hollywood received Gyssling' passage is a portrait of unusual accommodation — and C is the only option that names that accommodation in plain words.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read American filmmakers as initially defending their independence before later capitulating — a familiar 'eventual surrender' narrative.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says explicitly that 'instead of showing Gyssling the door' — i.e. instead of resisting — Hollywood went out of its way to cooperate. No initial opposition is described; step 2's first sentence rules out the narrative.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the later passage about the Hays Office and self-censorship and conclude that Gyssling's direct impact was modest.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says projects were dropped specifically 'because of fear of his disapproval' — that is direct impact, not modest impact. Step 5's option-by-option test catches the underweighting.

distractors.why_tempting: If you anchor on 'Germany as one of its biggest export markets', you might read Gyssling's mission as economic diplomacy.
distractors.why_wrong: Gyssling's activities were CENSORSHIP, not trade-promotion. The financial ties were Hollywood's reason for accommodating him; they were not his stated purpose. Step 4 keeps the purpose-versus-motive distinction visible.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-037.technique: On 'What is said about X's activities with regard to Y?' questions, the correct answer is almost always Y's RECEPTION of X — how Y reacted, treated, or responded. Find the sentences that describe Y's attitude (verbs like 'listened', 'went out of', 'avoided'), and paraphrase that attitude in plain language.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: It is tempting to read Hollywood as the resistant party in any Nazi-era story — the moral default. The text deliberately upends that default; the right move is to stay with the passage's literal claims even when they are uncomfortable.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The text says studios 'agreed not to attack the Nazis' and 'decided not to defend the Jews' — references to Dreyfus as a Jew were removed, and Jewish identity in The Mortal Storm was cut. Across multiple films, Hollywood refused to stand up for Jews. That is exactly B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks which option is MOST in agreement with the text. That means: which option restates a claim the text actually makes, while the other options contradict, exaggerate, or invent claims. Match each option literally to specific sentences.

steps.title: Locate the relevant evidence
steps.text: Three pieces of evidence converge. (i) 'The Hollywood studios not only agreed not to attack the Nazis but they also decided not to defend the Jews.' (ii) The Life of Emile Zola — 'references to Alfred Dreyfus being a Jew were removed.' (iii) The Mortal Storm — 'all references to the fact that they were Jews were cut from the final version.' Plus: 'many of the studio bosses were themselves Jewish immigrants.'

steps.title: Paraphrase the pattern
steps.text: Across multiple films and across years, Hollywood systematically refused to take a public stand on behalf of Jews — removing identifications, dropping anti-Nazi projects, going along with the consul. The pattern is consistent: a policy of refusal to defend, even from studios run by Jewish immigrants.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Decided not to defend' = a deliberate choice not to advocate. 'References were removed' = active editing to delete Jewish identity from films. 'Consistently refused to take a stand' = pattern over time, not a one-off. The text gives both the policy statement AND the case evidence.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'some film people were antisemites without being Nazis' — the text never describes Hollywood figures as personal antisemites; it describes a policy of self-censorship, often by Jewish bosses. B 'consistently refused to take a stand on behalf of the Jews' matches 'decided not to defend the Jews' plus the Zola and Mortal Storm edits — a pattern, not isolated cases. C 'antisemitism did not show its true face until well into the war' — the text describes pressure starting in 1933 and Kristallnacht in 1938; the face was visible long before the war. D 'Jews treated almost as badly in Hollywood as in Nazi Germany' — wild exaggeration; the text is about silence and self-censorship, not persecution.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The text makes a general policy claim ('decided not to defend the Jews') and backs it with two named films. B is the option that mirrors both the policy and its 'consistently' character.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'agreed not to attack the Nazis' as implying covert sympathy with Nazi ideology — i.e. some Hollywood figures being antisemites themselves.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly says many studio bosses were Jewish immigrants, framing the silence as commercial accommodation, not ideological alignment. Step 4 distinguishes self-censorship from personal antisemitism.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'after the Kristallnacht pogrom against Jews in 1938... distribution continued in 1940 and 1941', it can feel like the war was the moment of revelation.
distractors.why_wrong: The text describes 1933 bans of Jewish-starring films and 1938 blacklists — antisemitism was openly visible long before the war. The studios' silence in the face of obvious antisemitism is the story, not delayed recognition.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a Nazi-era passage is to reach for the strongest moral judgement — and 'almost as badly' captures outrage.
distractors.why_wrong: The text is about cinematic silence and edits, not about persecuting people. There is no claim that Hollywood Jews were physically endangered. D wildly overshoots the passage's actual claims.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-038.technique: On 'which is most in agreement with the text?' items, look for the option that mirrors a POLICY STATEMENT plus two CASE EXAMPLES. The text usually states a general claim and then backs it with two named instances — and the correct option is the one that captures both layers.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: Strong moral framing in WWII-era passages tempts readers to import their own conclusions. The 'most in agreement' criterion is literal — pick what the text says, even when the truth on the page is less satisfying than a stronger condemnation.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: Urwand titled the book The Collaboration, implying Hollywood collaborated with Hitler — and 'created a storm in America', with The New Yorker demanding to know how Harvard could publish it. The offence comes from people seeing the title as a false accusation against Hollywood — exactly D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The question asks for THE MAIN REASON the title was perceived as offensive. So look for the sentence that explains the OUTRAGE — who was outraged and why — and pin the answer to that explanation, not to the broader story.

steps.title: Locate the outrage explanation
steps.text: Two sentences sit together. 'By calling his book The Collaboration and implying that Hollywood collaborated with Hitler, Urwand has certainly created a storm in America.' 'The New Yorker film critic was so outraged that he has demanded to know how Harvard University Press could have published the book.'

steps.title: Paraphrase the offence
steps.text: The title accuses Hollywood of COLLABORATION — a word with strong wartime moral weight, implying knowing partnership with Nazi Germany. American readers (notably The New Yorker critic) see this accusation as unfair to the American film industry — which is the kind of strong claim they want a Harvard imprint to refuse. So the offence is the false-accusation reading.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Collaboration' (in WWII vocabulary) = active cooperation with an occupying enemy — used about Vichy France, Quisling Norway. Calling Hollywood collaborators is therefore a very heavy charge. 'Created a storm' = generated controversy. 'Demanded to know how Harvard could publish' = wants the prestigious imprint to disown the accusation.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'Hollywood only followed the lead of American foreign policy' — the text never argues this as a defence; it is not offered as a reason the title offends. B 'unfortunate translation of a specific German expression' — Zusammenarbeit is mentioned but the text says it MEANS 'collaboration'; the translation is accurate, not unfortunate. C 'Hollywood's self-censorship had little connection with Nazi pressure' — the text actually links the two; not offered as the source of offence. D 'some people see it as a false accusation of the American film industry' — matches 'created a storm' + 'outraged' + 'demanded to know how Harvard could publish'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The outrage comes from the perception that calling Hollywood 'collaborators' is an unfair indictment — and D names that perception directly.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to import a defence of Hollywood — 'they were following US policy' — as if the passage were debating whether the accusation is just.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't make that defence on Hollywood's behalf; it just reports that critics found the title offensive. The MAIN REASON is the offence itself, not a counter-argument the text never makes.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Zusammenarbeit, i.e. collaboration' and read the title as a translation problem.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly equates Zusammenarbeit with 'collaboration' — the translation is presented as accurate, not as unfortunate. The offence is the WORD, not its translation.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading of 'self-censorship by Hollywood' separated from Nazi pressure could suggest critics felt the link was overstated.
distractors.why_wrong: The text presents Hollywood self-censorship and Nazi pressure as directly connected (Hays Office, Gyssling). The outrage in the text is about the WORD 'collaboration', not about a contested causal link.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-039.technique: On 'main reason something has been regarded as offensive' questions, look for the OUTRAGE sentence — usually a sentence with a named outraged party (here The New Yorker critic). The reason is whatever that party is upset about — almost always quoted or paraphrased in the same sentence.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: It is easy to import a logical defence of the offended party ('they were doing X for reason Y') and read it as the source of offence. The offence sentence is usually about a CHARACTERISATION people reject, not about a defence they advance.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The reviewer calls Urwand's claim about Triumph of the Will 'bizarre' — a specific example used to ILLUSTRATE that Urwand sometimes mischaracterizes things. So the film is cited as an instance of Urwand getting it wrong — exactly B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks why the reviewer brings up Triumph of the Will. So find the surrounding sentences — what claim is the example serving, what verdict does the reviewer give it, and which option captures that role.

steps.title: Locate the framing sentence
steps.text: The example sits inside this frame: 'Urwand's book is thoroughly researched but is rather rambling, occasionally repetitious and makes some strange statements. For instance, it is bizarre to claim that Leni Riefenstahl's propaganda film Triumph of the Will (1935) is about a Hitler speech. It is a visual evocation of a creed...'

steps.title: Paraphrase the role
steps.text: The reviewer has just called Urwand's book 'rambling' and full of 'strange statements'. 'For instance' tells you Triumph of the Will is an EXAMPLE of those strange statements — specifically, an Urwand claim the reviewer finds wrong. So the film is brought up to show that Urwand misdescribes things — a single instance illustrating a wider mischaracterization problem.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Bizarre' = strange to the point of being wrong. 'For instance' = providing a specific example of the previous claim. 'Strange statements' = the headline criticism that the example is meant to support. The whole rhetorical move is 'critique → for instance → wrong example' — and Triumph of the Will is the wrong example.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A 'evidence of Hollywood's collaboration with Nazi filmmakers' — the film is a Nazi propaganda film, not a Hollywood production; not about Hollywood collaboration. B 'a specific case where Urwand's characterization is misleading' matches 'For instance, it is bizarre to claim...' — the film is the specific case of an Urwand misclaim. C 'Hollywood's dubious attitude to censorship' — the example is about a Nazi propaganda film, not Hollywood censorship. D 'symptomatic of Urwand's consistent disregard of historical facts' — too strong; the reviewer calls Urwand 'thoroughly researched' overall, and uses the example to flag occasional strange statements, not a consistent pattern.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The 'For instance, it is bizarre...' structure makes the film one specific case of an Urwand mischaracterization — exactly what B says, with B's 'specific case' echoing 'For instance' precisely.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading on a Hollywood/Nazi article can pull any named film into the 'collaboration' frame.
distractors.why_wrong: Triumph of the Will is a GERMAN propaganda film, not a Hollywood production. The example is about Urwand's description of it, not about cross-industry collaboration. Step 2's framing sentence locates the example clearly.

distractors.why_tempting: If you anchor on 'censorship' as the article's umbrella, almost any film example feels like it should slot under that theme.
distractors.why_wrong: Triumph of the Will is a Nazi propaganda film made FOR Nazi purposes — Hollywood censorship is not at issue. The example sits inside a paragraph about Urwand's writing, not about Hollywood policy.

distractors.why_tempting: Once you see 'bizarre to claim', it's tempting to escalate to 'consistent disregard of historical facts'.
distractors.why_wrong: The same paragraph calls Urwand's book 'thoroughly researched' — i.e. mostly factually solid. The reviewer flags occasional strange statements, not a consistent pattern of disregard. Step 5 catches the overstatement.

var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-040.technique: On 'Why is X mentioned by the reviewer?' questions, find the SENTENCE-BEFORE-X — the claim the example is meant to illustrate. The example almost always serves one specific point ('rambling', 'strange statements', 'thoroughly researched'). Match that role to the option whose framing verb echoes 'For instance' or 'an example of'.
var-2022-2-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: It is tempting to upgrade a single 'bizarre' example into a global verdict on the book. The reviewer is usually balancing praise and complaint — escalating one example to 'consistent disregard' overshoots and gets eliminated.
